item.dc:creator: LeonSpinks

item.title: Class struggle & inflation
item.description: Why Does Life Feel Harder, Even As We “Progress”? Every year, there's a new device, platform, or service claiming to make life easier. On paper, we're wealthier, more connected, more advanced than ever. And yet—for most people—life feels harder. Not easier. Not freer. Just more expensive, more demanding, and more uncertain. A smartphone is no longer a luxury; it’s a lifeline. Basic needs like healthcare and housing exist in theory but are increasingly unaffordable in practice. You can work a full-time job—sometimes two—and still not have enough to feel secure. It feels like we’re sprinting just to stay in place. And somehow, we’re told this is freedom. But this isn’t a glitch in the system. It is the system. We’ve come to accept a kind of performative progress, where innovation serves capital far more than it serves people. Even the moments that seem promising—calls for a living wage, expanded access to education, or basic health services—end up warped by deeper structural forces. Because the system isn’t just economic. It’s cultural. It’s psychological. Sociologist Pierre Bourdieu argued that class isn’t just about income—it’s about deeply internalized habits, expectations, and social positioning. What we consider "normal" or "successful" is shaped by a hierarchy we’ve all been trained to navigate. Even if we raised the standard of living for everyone, that hierarchy wouldn’t disappear. It would simply reinvent itself. This is where positional goods come in—things like elite universities, exclusive real estate, or luxury experiences. These aren’t valuable because of what they are, but because of who can’t access them. And when more people gain access to previously exclusive goods, the wealthy don’t stay put—they move the goalposts. They create new levels of exclusivity, pushing inflation into these spaces to preserve their separation. That’s not an accident. It’s a defense mechanism. Research on economic segregation and gentrification shows this clearly: when lower- or middle-income people start gaining ground, the market responds by re-segregating itself. New layers of cost, competition, or credentialing emerge—not because they’re necessary, but because they’re effective at keeping people in their place. Inequality adapts. Scarcity is manufactured. And dignity remains a luxury. Behavioral economists like Mullainathan and Shafir have shown how scarcity itself becomes a trap. When you’re constantly juggling costs, making trade-offs, or worrying about the next bill, your mental bandwidth narrows. You can't plan, can’t rest, can’t dream. It's not about laziness or bad decisions. It's about survival mode. And the system depends on millions of people being stuck there. Meanwhile, at the top, wealth has become less about labor or innovation and more about leverage—moving money between accounts, manipulating assets, inflating valuations, and calling it progress. Most wealth today isn’t real in the tangible sense. It’s speculative, symbolic—a game of numbers that generates nothing and produces value for no one but the already-rich. So while politicians from both parties offer slogans and culture war distractions, the real issues—financialization, monopolization, structural inequality—go unaddressed. They talk big, but act small. Because confronting the system would mean changing who it’s designed to serve. And neither party seems ready for that. But here’s what I believe: the real divide in this country isn’t left versus right. It’s between those who want control—and those who just want a fair shot at a decent life. Most of us aren’t asking for handouts or revolutions. We’re asking for something far more modest: sanity. Fairness. A system that doesn’t punish you for being poor or reward you for hoarding power. If you feel politically homeless, you’re not alone. There are millions of us caught in the middle—tired of being told to hustle harder, bootstrap more, or be patient while the world burns around us. Maybe it’s time to stop waiting for the system to fix itself. Maybe the real progress begins when we stop looking up for answers and start looking sideways—at each other. At what we could build if we stopped playing by their rules and started writing new ones, grounded in common sense, shared humanity, and the radical idea that life should not feel like a punishment.
item.dc:creator: edieburns

item.title: What Happened Today #68
item.description: Beneath the surface, norms are rotting. Trump’s crackdown isn’t random. It’s targeted, strategic, and retroactive. From deporting legally admitted migrants to punishing law firms and gutting civil rights oversight, this is authoritarianism cloaked in legalese. The goal? Instill fear, reward loyalty, and rewrite the rules beneath our feet. Use 5 Calls. Use Resistbot. Use Mobilize to find out where to show up locally. Democracy depends on active, constant resistance. Subscribe free at https://govbrief.today #GovBriefToday #Resist 1. https://abcnews.go.com/Politics/supreme-court-allows-trump-terminate-16000-probationary-federal/story?id=120599910 2. https://apnews.com/live/stock-market-economy-tariffs-updates-4-9-2025 3. https://federalnewsnetwork.com/people/2025/04/internal-revenue-service-agrees-to-send-immigrant-tax-data-to-ice-for-enforcement/ 4. https://www.msn.com/en-ie/news/world/pentagon-official-us-military-has-no-authority-to-do-drone-strikes-on-drug-cartels-in-mexico/ar-AA1CypSM 5. https://www.bbc.com/news/articles/ce9281ljxj0o 6. https://www.npr.org/2025/04/09/g-s1-59090/trump-officials-halt-1-billion-in-funding-for-cornell-790-million-for-northwestern 7. https://www.politico.com/news/2025/04/08/trump-reinstate-associated-press-00279868 8. https://www.cbsnews.com/newyork/news/judge-demands-evidence-against-mahmoud-khalil/ 9. https://time.com/7275841/trump-administration-fema-cancels-grants-new-york-city-shelter-migrants/ 10. https://www.usatoday.com/story/news/politics/2025/04/08/cryptocurrency-fraud-unit-shut-down/82991750007/ 11. https://thehill.com/homenews/race-politics/5238380-national-park-service-restores-tubman/ 12. https://www.star-telegram.com/news/state/texas/article303740486.html 13. https://www.eenews.net/articles/trump-inks-order-to-resurrect-us-coal/ 14. https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-04-09/about-90-of-migrants-sent-to-salvador-lacked-us-criminal-record 15. https://www.theguardian.com/australia-news/2025/apr/09/donald-trump-flags-major-tariff-on-pharmaceuticals-as-us-senator-criticises-whack-on-australia 16. https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-04-09/us-grades-el-salvador-as-safer-to-visit-than-france?srnd=phx-politics 17. https://www.wired.com/story/dr-oz-ai-health-care-medicare-cms-town-hall/ 18. https://www.npr.org/2025/04/08/g-s1-58984/cbp-one-app-migrants-dhs-border 19. https://www.rawstory.com/trump-law-firms-2671697770/

item.title: Where the Crafties at?
item.description:
Hey beautiful community!!
I'm hoping to get some DIY, cute gift ideas for my husband's and my anniversary in 3 weeks. Main issue is that I live in a place where getting some art supplies is difficult, so the simpler- the better! I'll happily fill in more details below, but in the meantime, have some lovey dovey memes I've stolen over the years
This is our 3rd year anniversary, so far I'm planning to make a decorative glass jar that has lights and fake sunflowers, maybe some fake pearls if I can find some nice ones, and a love letter sealed with a wax seal (We've been binge watching TaskMaster, so I may also do some cutesy tasks) but I feel I'm lacking a certain umph.
I try to get as many of the different types of gifts into it as possible (traditional, current, flower, color, and stone) and this year has been a doozy so I wasn't able to secure the leather belts I had intended so I'm a bit disheartened...
The first years wedding anniversary I made a shadow box featuring items such as a flower I made with Oragami out of some of the paper from one of the presents he gave me for Christmas the first year we dated, a mock newspaper headline article I had written and had printed out talking about our wedding day and some other sweet trinkets.
Our second anniversary I made us a deck of playing cards from pressed cotton with a photo of us on them (we play cribbage often), a beautiful wooden card box with our date on it, a towel with the same, and at the end of the day- surprised him with a card saying something like "even after all these years, I still see fireworks when you walk into the room" with a handful of sparklers for us to light off.
I had known what I wanted to do for the leather anniversary from the first year when I had checked the lists (it was supposed to be matching leather belts with sunflowers and some special words written on them) but this hasn't worked out and I'm pretty upset, but now I'll save that for a birthday gift or something along those lines.
I already cook all of the time, and we will have a nice homemade breakfast and then go out for supper. I'll probably also make some fudge, so food isn't my angle, though I do always love to be sent recipes no matter what :)
Yeah, there will be as many of these as I can possibly create hidden around the house as well :), most likely with little notes on them
Sorry for such a long blurb, I'm just really trying to make this anniversary as special as the others. Usually I'm able to mail all my items to my moms in my birth country and bring them back with me when I visit for the holidays which didn't happen, so this year is relatively a struggle...and thank you ahead of time to anyone that actually reads this, let alone anyone that sends ideas <3
I hope you all have or will have someone you love even an ounce of the amount that I luv my hubs :)
Sleeping Gandalf tax
item.dc:creator: kittenspacecadet
